text_clean: 😉Fue un deseo de visitar a la Montaña Huaynapicchu y ver parte de las ruinas del gran imperio inca, como dicen por ahí la montaña es un reto mas aun cuando esta en épocas de lluvia el camino es difícil y muy peligroso, pero no me importo por que escalaba esa montaña en el día de mi cumpleaños; tuve una gran suerte de que mi agente turística estaba muy pendiente de mi y se las recomendare busque a la señora Linda Illatupa su Whatsapp es 939177232, hagan presente mi nombre para que reciban una rebaja en sus tours los demás que pululan por la plaza te incrementan cuando no conoces los precios. Lo único malo y no es por parte de los agentes turísticos es el problema del tren mi agente en todo momento me decía que ni bien baje del bus de retorno inmediatamente reservar tu boleto del tren si no están pendientes uds. mismo pueden perder el tren y quedarse un día en el pueblo y el hostal mas barato esta 120 soles y la comida ni que decir, el tren algunas veces llega a llevar solo 400 o 500 pasajeros y eso si sacas con tiempo tu ticket de retorno solo hay 3 salidas del tren 2 5 y 9 de la noche, en ese dia que viaje era el numero 160 y mi retorno estaba programado a las 5:00pm y solo dijeron que del 30 al 200 subirán al tren y el resto turno de las 9:00pm de la noche ese mismo día a promediar 9:50pm cae una fuerte lluvia con crecida de rio y hubo derrumbe en esa zona hubo 3 desaparecidos y las demás personas quedaron varados todo esto me entere en la mañana con mi agente de turismo pues el decía que si abordaría en el tren y otras personas que estaban con ella mucha no llegaron en ese día....pero como dice lo vivido nadie me lo quita nada mas hermoso de conocer dicha maravilla👍

text_clean: El pueblo de aguas Calientes me pareció hermoso, me parece que es un lugar bastante hermoso pero exageradamente costoso y con un poco de falta de cultura, al caminar pude ver cómo un lugar de estos que es patrimonio de la humanidad se encuentra lleno de basura y contaminación tanto por los locales como por los mismos turistas que van a visitar a este bello lugar, también es un lugar en el que todo te lo quieren cobrar en dólares y conseguir boletería es bastante complejo y difícil de conseguir, en ocasiones no se consigue ni estando en el mismo lugar, mi forma de ver a Machu Picchu fue el subir una de las montañas que está al frente, una ruta hermosa pero algo complicada debido a que cortaron los anclajes y escaleras, solo quedan dos cuerdas y dos Guayas que sirven de ayuda el resto toca escalar bajo su propio riesgo. La forma fácil de ir por su propia cuenta es comprar un tiquete en la terminal terrestre que va a Quillabamba y tienes que bajarte en santa María y que tiene un valor de 25 soles por persona, el bus se toma en la terminal de Quillabamba y un taxi te cobra 6 soles, en santa María se toma un colectivo hasta santa Teresa que te cobrará 10 soles por persona y de santa Teresa a hidro electrica te cobrarán 5 soles por persona y de ahí solo queda caminar hasta llegar al pueblo de aguas calientes.

text_clean: Machu Pichhu Visit – A Truly Unforgettable Experience! Our trip to Machu Pichhu, organised by our friend Shoba Kalyan from Canada, was perfectly planned and combined with tours in Argentina and Brazil. We stayed in comfortable 4-star hotels and had excellent guides throughout. We arrived in Cusco after a delayed flight and took Diamox to handle the high altitude (Cusco is 11,200 ft above sea level). The city tour was fascinating, with beautiful Inca ruins and colonial charm. From Ollantaytambo, we took a scenic 1 hour 40-minute train ride to Machu Pichhu town. The station was crowded and the platform low, but our hotel helped with luggage transport, which was a relief. Machu Pichhu itself is incredible—an ancient 15th-century Inca citadel with amazing stone terraces, temples aligned with the stars, and stunning mountain views. Our guide was knowledgeable, explaining the site’s history, engineering, and spiritual significance. The weather turned rainy and misty during our trek, which made walking tricky, so I highly recommend bringing a good raincoat even in the dry season. Despite the rain, the experience was magical and well worth it. Highly recommend visiting Machu Pichhu with a good guide and proper preparation

text_clean: Un lugar mágico y espléndido. Vale la pena ir. El mejor circuito es el #2, largo y te da varias vistas. Nosotros ingresamos a las 6 am y fue muy fácil, mejor llegar en tren y no caminando, la ruta del Inca es de 2 horas ida y 2 horas vuelta. Fuimos en Octubre y era temporada baja, el mismo día que llegamos a Aguas calientes compramos el boleto de ingreso a Machu sin reserva, sin problemas, hay un descuento para los colombianos por ser comunidad Andina. Si viajas en tren nos gustó más la experiencia con Inca Rail, tienen un bonito servicio, en el viaje nos dieron onces y tienen un vagón con música en vivo. Recomendación: el tiquete de regreso es mejor comprarlo 1 día antes, se agotan rápido. El hospedaje en Aguas Calientes es fácil de conseguir pero se puede mirar ofertas por Apps. En Perú es mejor madrugar para todo, rinde más el tiempo. El pasaje en tren y el bus para llegar a Machu son caros, el promedio fue de 250 dólares por persona para cubrir, 1 noche hospedaje, 1 dia de visita, 6 comidas, todo el transporte en modo económico (tren ida y vuelta desde Cusco a Aguas Calientes y vcsa por Inca Rail, bus desde Aguas Calientes hasta Machu ida y vuelta, solo hay una empresa, 1 noche en hotel para 2 personas y 6 comidas para 2 días, presupuesto por persona.

text_clean: The site itself is exactly what you see in the photos. The views around are nice. It's very crowded though, and buying tickets is a bureaucratic nightmare: You have to queue for a number that allows you to buy a ticket at a certain time. Then have to come back at that time, listen for your number (which is called out by some guy in Spanish to a crowd of hundreds), then queue again. Then you have to pick one of 6 routes and 3 additions, most of which will no longer be available in the upcoming days, depending on your luck. The routes that are available usually don't allow entrance to the actual site since those are booked way in advance via a terrible online system. If you want to go at the start of the year, you cannot book tickets in advance, because you can only by tickets for the current year. Also, this online system is down or unresponsive for most of January. Even though we booked through an agency 6 months in advance, they were not able to secure our tickets so we had to queue. In the end, our group were not able to actually visit the site, only take a photo from top.... There are so many nice things to see in Peru and South America... Including much larger and more impressive Inca settlements, which are much cheaper or even free to visit... It's really a shame the Peruvian makes it so difficult to visit this place. Unless you really really want to visit Machu Picchu, I would recommend spending your time elsewhere.

text_clean: Machu Picchu fue espectacular! Acá algunas consideraciones para que tu visita sea placentera: - Antes de tu visita a Machu Picchu, compra tus entradas por su web (es la única vía para comprar las entradas) con varias semanas de anticipación, ya que se acaban muy rápido. Si no las compras con tiempo, corres el riesgo de tener que hacer cola en Aguas Calientes y gastar más de lo tenías previsto. - Una vez que llegues a Aguas Calientes, ve a la oficina de venta de Tickets para el bus. El pasaje cuesta 15 dólares ida y vuelta y tienes la opción de pagarlo en soles al tipo de cambio del día. Puedes pagar en efectivo o tarjeta. - Los paraderos de buses a Machu Picchu tienen unos letreros con unos horarios, el cual indican la hora de entrada a Machu Picchu, por lo que tienes que estar 1 hora antes haciendo cola para el bus. - En la entrada a Machu Picchu hay baño a 2 soles y guardarropa a 5 soles. - Te recomiendo que vayas con un guía que te ayudará con tu recorrido. text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.

text_clean: Hermoso lugar, lleno de una gran y especial energía. Nosotros contratamos una agencia, lo que facilitó todo el viaje. Incluso pudimos dejar las mochilas con un encargado que las llevó a nuestro hotel de Aguas Calientes. Así subimos livianos, solo con lo necesario. Tuvimos una guía, Eli de agencia Pacifico, que nos dio una charla maravillosa de cómo vivieron en esa linda ciudad. Y a mediodía nos dejó libres para que recorramos a nuestras anchas. A las 2 almorzamos y todo ok. Lo único es la espera para poder bajar. La fila para tomar los buses llegaba hasta la curva del camino, pero es solo paciencia. Es un viaje 100% recomendado.

It's the most secure place because is where the Town Hall and I guess the parlament is placed. The square is a World Heritage Site, declared by UNESCO, and so then is forbidden to protest and make political acts. All this acts can be done in another square very closed to the Main Square. Thats is why the square is so clean as well. At midday you can see the Changing of the Guards but it's not something big. It's just a cool formality. I enjoyed been there because is a historical place too. Many important events happened there and during the Football World Cup the watched the matches there. It's a place that you should visit!

text_clean: 6-7 hour bus ride from Arequipa (terminal terrestre, Arturo Ibanez 130) to Cabanaconde. Route over highlands, vicuna reserve, down to Chivay in canyon, then down canyon, (best views from right side of bus). 9:30 AM Arequipa departure (Andelusia) and nights in Cabanaconde before and after self guided trek, seemed better than 3:00 AM Arequipa departure, short last trek day and back to Arequipa of tours. Many places to stay, I was at Pachamama hostel about 2 blocks up from the plaza on San Pedro St. Good information about trek options and good place to meet others doing unguided treks. I did 2d/1n trek, 1st day to Llahuar with scenic descent along canyon edge, then down into side canyon, final descent to Colca river crossing, climb into Taruna, back down to Llahuar on the Colca river just after crossing the Huaruro river. Nice bungalows on river, friendly staff, fixed menu dinner, breakfast and sandwichs available, all at very reasonable prices. Natural hot pools and swimming pool right on river not open because river very high in early March. Very ambitious new "restaurant" construction ongoing. As hike to Llahuar mostly down and not too long, I was there well before noon. Could just chill in nice location. I opted for day hike up Huaruro canyon that starts steeply right from Llahuar then more gradually up to Llatica where a bridge crosses the river. From there another steep climb to high trail that contours back down other side of canyon to above Belen. Nice safe and scenic canyon trails. Down main street in Belen past soccer field to steep trail to Paclla. Cross road and continue on trail down through terraced gardens that eventually goes to Taruna above Llahuar. This added another 11 km and 700m vertical, about 4 hours at a fast pace, to first day. 2nd day back up to Belen, options are trail short cuts to Paclla not too steep, then very steep to Belen, or longer but more gradually walk on road. From Belen you are on road up to viewpoint bend then level to shortcut trail down to plateau, join main trail down to Sangalle (relatively touristy and expensive, but amazing given that everything has to come in by horse or burro). That just leaves the 2.5 - 3.5 hour all uphill switchbacks to plateau and final km to Cabanaconde. Total for 2nd day a healthy 16-18km with 1700m elevation gain. If I was doing it again, would spend 2 nights in canyon, 1st at Llahuar, then 2nd up at Fure with day hike up to the waterfall (cataratas), day 3 high trail back from Fure contouring back to join road at high point above Sangalle, then through Sangalle up and out. Another interesting high altitude strenuous option might be continuing up from Fure to Tocallo at 3800m where some maps show a trail that goes in and out of several canyons with final pass then long descent to Malata above Sangalle. Another night in canyon and some local information about condition of trails would be needed. Canyon country was unique, scenic and very green with lots of water in the rainy season. Lodging and meals in many areas down in canyon allows for very light packs, a plus given all the climbs. No reservations needed for anything during rainy season, probably a different story in high season. A minibus service that goes on the not too scary dirt road from Carbanaconde, crossing the Rio Colca at the same place my trek did has beautiful brand new rain sheltered stops at all the little towns all the way to Tapay! This allows some less strenuous options for the canyon that are sure to become more popular.

text_clean: The sights here are are amazing I would tell anyone that should see it once in their lifetime at least I especially enjoyed it as my bloodline traces back to Incan blood. That being said it is a tourist spot there are large amounts of people entering throughout the day you are walking and crowds the people out there don't have respect for personal space we'll bump into you cut you as you're trying to climb up and making things with a semi dangerous. I was part of a tour guide in the future if I go I will probably hire a personal tour guide as one of us in our group was elderly and could not keep up with the group. Something to keep in mind if you're going with a parent as I did also another thing to think about is Altitude pills I got some for my doctor before I went out there started taking it the day before I got to Cusco and I did not have an issues others who did not do this had issues after explaining my gripe I would most definitely return and plan to one day and I hope I take more pictures next time I go.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
